misc.title: Maybe the reason Gunko is always wrapped in those arrow-bandages is because her runny nose still hasn’t stopped even NOW 🤧 And by the way, here’s a bit of Lost in Translation from a native Japanese p
misc.text: Maybe the reason Gunko is always wrapped in those arrow-bandages is because her runny nose still hasn’t stopped even NOW 🤧 And by the way, here’s a bit of Lost in Translation from a native Japanese perspective. Shuri says: "しとづまに恋するのはまずいよブルック" “Falling for a married woman is bad, Brook.” Here, hitozuma (“married woman”) is pronounced shitozuma, and I’ve seen some English-speaking posts pointing to that as a mistake. But from a native speaker’s perspective, that’s not really how it comes across. When someone has a stuffy nose, it’s actually pretty common for “hi” not to come out clearly and sound more like “shi” instead. So for Shuri, who’s literally dripping snot, to pronounce it that way is completely fitting.

items.title: Maybe the reason Gunko is always wrapped in those arrow-bandages is because her runny nose still hasn’t stopped even NOW 🤧 And by the way, here’s a bit of Lost in Translation from a native Japanese p
items.text: Maybe the reason Gunko is always wrapped in those arrow-bandages is because her runny nose still hasn’t stopped even NOW 🤧 And by the way, here’s a bit of Lost in Translation from a native Japanese perspective. Shuri says: "しとづまに恋するのはまずいよブルック" “Falling for a married woman is bad, Brook.” Here, hitozuma (“married woman”) is pronounced shitozuma, and I’ve seen some English-speaking posts pointing to that as a mistake. But from a native speaker’s perspective, that’s not really how it comes across. When someone has a stuffy nose, it’s actually pretty common for “hi” not to come out clearly and sound more like “shi” instead. So for Shuri, who’s literally dripping snot, to pronounce it that way is completely fitting.
